purpose: Phaser 3 bug patterns and gotchas to harden code-generation prompt against amateur mistakes. Each entry has a wrong pattern, a right pattern, and a one-line prompt directive. | 2026-05-05: extended with 12 anti-patterns from Vector Storm playtest (hit-stop multi-kill freeze, tween-on-destroyed, bomb-on-boss softlock, wave-timer race, per-bullet Graphics no-batching, etc.).

patterns.title: Body.reset() clobbers velocity set before it
patterns.description: Phaser.Physics.Arcade.Body.reset(x, y) sets the body to the new position AND zeroes velocity, acceleration, and speed. Calling setVelocity() BEFORE reset() means the velocity is silently destroyed. This is the most common amateur bug when respawning enemies/projectiles from a Group: you grab a sleeping member, set its velocity, then reset its position, and it appears stuck because reset just wiped the velocity you set.
patterns.prompt_directive: Always call body.reset(x, y) BEFORE setVelocity/setAcceleration; reset() zeroes motion. Re-apply allowGravity flags after reset on Group members.

patterns.title: Group.get() returns inactive sprite without resetting body state
patterns.description: Group.get() recycles a member that was disabled (kill pattern: setActive(false), setVisible(false), disableBody). The recycled body still has the old velocity, position, and disabled state. Amateurs forget to re-enable the body or expect the body to be 'fresh'. The right call is enableBody(true, x, y, true, true) which re-enables the body, repositions it, and shows the sprite in one shot.
patterns.prompt_directive: When recycling Group members, call enableBody(true, x, y, true, true) to reset position+visibility+active in one atomic call before setting velocity.

patterns.title: Adding a physics body to a child of a non-zero Container yields offset bodies
patterns.description: Phaser's Arcade physics intentionally does NOT factor Container transforms into child bodies (per official docs: 'Container children with physics do not factor in the Container due to the excessive extra calculations needed'). If your Container is positioned anywhere except 0,0, child sprites with physics bodies render at one location but their bodies are at another. Symptom: collisions fire in empty space.
patterns.prompt_directive: Never put physics-enabled sprites inside a Container that is not at (0,0). Use this.add.layer() or a plain array for grouping physics sprites; Containers are visual-only.

patterns.title: Container.x/y is local, not world; child.x is local to the container
patterns.description: Container's transform point is fixed at [0,0] local space. A child sprite's .x and .y are relative to the Container origin. To get world coordinates of a child, use child.getWorldTransformMatrix() and read .tx/.ty, or container.localTransform.transformPoint(child.x, child.y). Amateurs use child.x as world position, leading to camera follow targets, particle spawn points, and collision tests being wrong.
patterns.prompt_directive: Container child .x/.y are LOCAL coords. For world position use child.getWorldTransformMatrix().tx/.ty. Prefer following the Container itself for camera tracking.

patterns.title: this.sound.play(key) replays from start instead of overlapping
patterns.description: this.sound.play('shoot') uses a single shared Sound instance; rapid-fire calls cut off the previous play. For overlapping rapid-fire SFX (laser, hit, coin), each call needs a separate Sound instance. Use this.sound.add(key) to create instances, or pool them. For HTML5 audio, you must declare instances at load time: this.load.audio('shoot', url, { instances: 4 }).
patterns.prompt_directive: For rapid-fire SFX use a small pool of this.sound.add(key) instances and round-robin them; for HTML5 audio set { instances: N } at load time.

patterns.title: Background music duplicates on scene.restart()
patterns.description: The Sound Manager is GLOBAL to the Game, not per-Scene. Calling this.sound.play('bgm', { loop: true }) in create() and then scene.restart() creates a second BGM instance; the original keeps looping. Symptom: every restart layers another music track over the previous. Fix: stop the existing instance before starting (or, better, store the music instance on the registry and only create it once).
patterns.prompt_directive: Sound Manager is GLOBAL: stop existing music (this.sound.stopByKey or stored ref) before starting new music in create(); always stop scene-owned music on SHUTDOWN event.

patterns.title: Confusing scene.start, scene.launch, scene.switch — and skipping cleanup
patterns.description: scene.start(key) STOPS the calling scene and starts the target. scene.launch(key) starts the target IN PARALLEL (caller keeps running, useful for HUD overlays). scene.switch(key) SLEEPS the caller and starts/wakes the target. A shutdown scene is not destroyed; only the DESTROY event fully clears resources. Amateurs use scene.launch when they meant scene.start and end up with two GameScenes running simultaneously, both processing input and physics.
patterns.prompt_directive: Use scene.start to replace, scene.launch only for parallel HUD overlays; always register a SHUTDOWN handler that stops timers, sounds, and removes event listeners added in create().

patterns.title: Single-frame state cascade — wave-cleared trigger fires every frame
patterns.description: When checking 'is wave cleared' in update() with a condition like (enemies.countActive() === 0 && !waveCompleted), if you forget to set waveCompleted=true BEFORE doing async-feeling work (spawning the next wave one tick later, awaiting a promise, etc.), the condition stays true on the very next frame and triggers again. Worse: a condition like (this.time.now - this.lastWaveAt > 5000) is true every frame after the threshold until you update lastWaveAt. This is the #1 cause of 'multiple wave spawns at once' or 'instant game over loop' bugs.
patterns.prompt_directive: Every update()-driven trigger needs a re-entry guard: set the flag/timestamp BEFORE the side effect, not after. Never assume scheduled callbacks fire before the next update tick.

patterns.title: Tweens, TimerEvents, and listeners leaked into next scene run
patterns.description: Tweens auto-destroy on completion BUT looping tweens, persistent tweens (yoyo+repeat:-1), TimerEvents, and external event listeners (this.events.on, this.input.on, this.scene.scene.events.on, registry.events.on) survive scene shutdown unless the Scene is fully torn down. After scene.restart() the new instance creates fresh ones; old ones still hold references to destroyed sprites. Symptoms: 'Cannot read property of null', 'this.frame.source is null on setText', and runaway memory growth.
patterns.prompt_directive: Save references to looping tweens, TimerEvents, and external emitter listeners; remove all of them in a SHUTDOWN handler registered with this.events.once.

patterns.title: Null texture key, missing frame name, animation referencing unloaded atlas
patterns.description: If a texture key is undefined, Phaser returns the __DEFAULT magenta texture. If the key exists but the frame name does not, you get __MISSING. Animations created with generateFrameNames/generateFrameNumbers silently skip non-existent frames; an empty animation plays nothing with no error. Common amateur mistakes: (1) creating animations in create() before the atlas finished loading in another scene, (2) using a frame name with a typo, (3) calling sprite.play('walk') with an anim key that was never registered.
patterns.prompt_directive: Load all atlases in a Preload scene (never in Game create); verify generateFrameNames returns non-empty before anims.create; guard sprite.play with this.anims.exists.

patterns.title: pointerdown/keydown firing twice across scene restarts and DOM/Phaser overlap
patterns.description: Three common causes: (1) listener added in create() but not removed in SHUTDOWN, then scene.restart() runs create() again and there are now TWO listeners; (2) DOM listener AND Phaser input both attached and both firing; (3) keydown event fires repeatedly while held — by default Phaser's keydown emits once per OS repeat, but if you bind your own emitter without checking event.repeat you can fire on every repeat. Mobile pause-during-pointerdown can drop the pointerup, leaving stuck pointers (Phaser issue #3887).
patterns.prompt_directive: For continuous-action triggers use Phaser.Input.Keyboard.JustDown in update; for event-style listeners always pair .on with .off in a SHUTDOWN handler.

patterns.title: HUD shows 'Score: NaN' or 'Score: undefined' on first frame or after restart
patterns.description: Two failure modes: (1) Text initialized with `'Score: ' + this.score` while this.score is undefined → 'Score: undefined'; subsequent +=1 yields NaN. (2) After scene.restart(), referencing a stale Text reference from a destroyed scene throws 'this.frame.source is null on setText' (Phaser issue #4323) because the previous scene's Text was destroyed but a global/registry holds the old reference.
patterns.prompt_directive: Initialize all HUD numeric state in init() with explicit defaults (?? 0), use template literals (not concatenation) in setText, and never store Text refs outside the Scene that owns them.

patterns.title: JSON.parse on null/corrupt save crashes scene; no schema migration
patterns.description: localStorage.getItem returns null when the key doesn't exist; JSON.parse(null) returns null (not throw), but JSON.parse('') and JSON.parse(corrupt) throw SyntaxError that crashes create(). Amateur saves omit a version field; when the schema changes (added a new field, renamed one) old saves break silently or crash. Storage may also be disabled (private mode, quota exceeded) — setItem throws QuotaExceededError.
patterns.prompt_directive: All localStorage I/O wrapped in try/catch; saves carry a version field; migrate() fills missing fields from a DEFAULT_SAVE; never assume getItem returned valid JSON.

patterns.title: Player drawn below collision layer / above foreground because creation order or depth wrong
patterns.description: Phaser renders Game Objects in display-list order (creation order) UNLESS depth is set explicitly. The conventional layer pattern is: ground -> objects/decoration -> player+enemies -> foreground/canopy. If you create the player BEFORE the ground layer, the player draws under it. setDepth() overrides the display-list order — use it for predictable z-stacking. Note: the .z property does NOT control 2D rendering, only .depth does.
patterns.prompt_directive: Set explicit setDepth on every tilemap layer and on the player; never rely on creation order for z-stacking; .z does NOT affect 2D rendering, only .depth does.

patterns.title: Camera follows target into the void; no world bounds set
patterns.description: By default camera bounds are 0,0 to canvas width/height — this clamps the camera to the visible canvas only. With a larger world, you must call camera.setBounds(0, 0, worldW, worldH) AND physics.world.setBounds(...) separately. Without camera bounds, startFollow makes the camera scroll into negative space when the player walks off-screen. Without physics bounds, the player can leave the world. These are TWO different APIs and amateurs forget one or the other.
patterns.prompt_directive: Always set BOTH physics.world.setBounds(...) AND cameras.main.setBounds(...) to the same world rect; call startFollow with lerp 0.1 for smooth tracking; player.setCollideWorldBounds(true).

patterns.title: Listener and tween over-subscription via repeated this.events.on across restarts
patterns.description: Each scene.restart() runs create() again. Any listener attached with this.events.on (or this.input.on, registry.events.on, or DOM addEventListener) without an off in SHUTDOWN accumulates linearly with restarts. After 10 restarts your damage handler runs 11 times per hit. Same with tween targets — orphaned tweens hold references to destroyed sprites and continue calling onUpdate. Particle emitters left running burn GPU.
patterns.prompt_directive: Cap particle quantity/frequency, store bound handler refs so .off can match them, register cleanup for ALL non-Phaser listeners (DOM, registry, external) in SHUTDOWN.

patterns.title: Scene data passed to scene.start vanishes after scene.restart()
patterns.description: scene.start('Game', { level: 3 }) delivers data to init(data). But scene.restart() (no args) passes nothing — init runs with undefined data. Reading data?.level then defaults to undefined and your level resets. Same trap with scene.launch — data is one-shot, not persistent. Persist cross-scene state in this.registry (Data Manager attached to the Game), not scene properties.
patterns.prompt_directive: Default destructure scene init data with `init(data = {})` and `data.x ?? fallback`; persist cross-restart state in this.registry, never in module-level vars.

patterns.title: Collider added inside update() — accumulates one new collider per frame
patterns.description: this.physics.add.collider(a, b) registers a persistent collider; it should run ONCE in create(). Calling it inside update() (or inside a per-frame branch) accumulates colliders, doubling, tripling, ... CPU time. Same trap with overlap. Symptom: game gets progressively slower; collision callbacks fire many times per single overlap event.
patterns.prompt_directive: Register all this.physics.add.collider/overlap pairs ONCE in create(); never inside update(), event handlers, or per-frame conditional branches.

patterns.title: Hit-stop captures stale timeScale → multi-kill freezes the game at slow-mo
patterns.description: The standard hit-stop pattern is `oldTS = world.timeScale; world.timeScale = 5; delayedCall(50, () => world.timeScale = oldTS)`. With a single kill it works, but with multi-kill frames (chain explosion, AOE, bomb, dense waves) kill #2 captures the value kill #1 just set (5), schedules a restore-TO-5, and the LAST callback wins → game stuck at timeScale=5 forever (i.e. permanent 5x slow-motion). Looks like a freeze, is actually slow-mo. Single most important Phaser bug class to gate against because it ONLY surfaces in chaotic moments.
patterns.prompt_directive: NEVER capture timeScale with `oldTS = world.timeScale` inside a kill callback — multi-kill frames pin it. Use `if (!this._hitStopActive)` flag, set timeScale to 5, schedule a restore-to-1 (literal 1, NOT a captured value), and clear the flag in the same callback.

patterns.title: Destroying a Graphics object mid-tween leaks the tween and freezes the renderer
patterns.description: If a tween is animating `enemy.graphics.scale` and the player kills the enemy mid-tween, calling `enemy.graphics.destroy()` leaves the tween writing to a destroyed object. Phaser sometimes tolerates this, sometimes throws, sometimes silently corrupts the tween manager — at scale (boss telegraph + fast kill = guaranteed) the renderer chokes. Same applies to score-popup tweens, banner tweens, bullet tweens, anything.
patterns.prompt_directive: Before destroy()ing any Graphics/Image/Sprite that may have an active tween, call `this.tweens.killTweensOf(target)`. Apply this in every cleanup helper (enemy death, satellite death, projectile expiry).

patterns.title: Clear-screen bombs that destroy bosses softlock the wave
patterns.description: A bomb that iterates all enemies and calls `_killEnemy(e)` works for regular enemies but silently breaks boss fights: it destroys the boss sprite without firing the boss-death pipeline (`_onBossDefeated()`, world-clear flag, music ramp, advance timer). `bossActive` stays true forever, the HP bar lingers, and the wave can't progress. User loses the bomb AND the run.
patterns.prompt_directive: Clear-screen bombs/AOE/special weapons that 'kill all enemies' MUST special-case bosses + satellites: deal partial damage (e.g., 25% maxHP) and route through the same death flow regular-bullet kills use (_onBossDefeated, satellite-removal, shield-down trigger). Never bypass the boss death pipeline.

patterns.title: Wave timer fires _advanceWave on the frame the boss dies → double-advance + chaos
patterns.description: Boss waves typically run for 30-60s. When the boss dies, `bossActive=false` and the next frame the wave-management code sees `waveElapsed > waveTimer` (because the timer kept ticking through the boss fight) and immediately calls `_advanceWave()`. Meanwhile, `_onBossDefeated()` queued a `delayedCall(2200ms, _advanceWave)` for the celebration. Both fire — wave counter advances TWICE, two burst-spawns happen in 2.2s, enemies pile up, world-music swap stomps itself, freeze.
patterns.prompt_directive: If your game has boss waves with timed advances, gate the wave-timer-based `_advanceWave()` call behind both `!this.bossActive` AND `!this._bossDeathPending`. Set the pending flag in `_onBossDefeated`, clear it at the top of `_advanceWave`.

patterns.title: One Graphics object per projectile breaks the renderer at 100+ bullets
patterns.description: Spawning bullets as `physics.add.sprite('_invisible') + this.add.graphics().lineBetween(...)` creates a separate Graphics GameObject per bullet. Each Graphics is its own draw call — not batched, geometry re-tessellated every frame. A QUAD-spread weapon with x4 stack fires 12 bullets per click at 8/sec = 96 bullets/sec, peaking ~190 alive at once. The display list grows by 100+ Graphics nodes, GPU chokes, framerate collapses. Pre-bake one texture per weapon tier ONCE, use `physics.add.image(x, y, 'bullet_tN')` — all bullets of the same tier share a texture and Phaser batches them into a single draw call.
patterns.prompt_directive: NEVER allocate a per-bullet Phaser.Graphics object. Pre-bake one texture per weapon tier in create() via `make.graphics({add:false}) + generateTexture(key, w, h)`, then use `physics.add.image(x, y, key)` for bullets. Phaser batches same-texture sprites; per-Graphics bullets do not batch and tank the renderer at 100+ projectiles.

patterns.title: Calling clear()+lineStyle()+strokeCircle() per frame on Graphics is way more expensive than tween-driven scale
patterns.description: Animated Graphics elements (damage rings, expanding shockwaves, black-hole vortices) often look like: `gfx.clear(); gfx.lineStyle(3, color, alpha); gfx.strokeCircle(0, 0, currentRadius)` every frame. That tessellates new geometry every tick — at 60 fps with N rings active, that's N×60 tessellations per second. The right pattern: draw the shape ONCE at radius=1 (or fixed size), then animate via `tweens.add({ targets: gfx, scale: maxRadius, alpha: 0, duration: 700 })`. Phaser tweens just write to the GameObject's transform — no re-tessellation, no draw-call overhead beyond the original shape.
patterns.prompt_directive: For animated Graphics (rings, vortices, expanding effects), draw the shape ONCE at unit size in the spawn function, then drive scale/alpha/rotation via Phaser tween. Never call clear() + redraw inside an update loop — re-tessellation per frame collapses framerate at scale.

patterns.title: One Text+tween per kill spams 30+ GameObjects per second at high multiplier
patterns.description: Score popups feel great visually but become a perf cliff. A chain-lightning kill cascade or rocket AOE can wipe 10 enemies in one frame; with QUAD bursts hitting a swarm, you can sustain 30+ kills/sec. Each spawns a Text object (font rendering, atlas) + a Phaser tween. The tween manager's tick cost grows linearly with active tweens. Cap concurrent popups (e.g., 6) — past that, skip the popup. The HUD multiplier counter pulses on every kill anyway, so feedback fidelity stays high.
patterns.prompt_directive: Cap concurrent score-popup Text objects at 6. Filter out destroyed entries before testing the cap. Skipping popups beyond the cap is fine — the HUD multiplier counter already provides per-kill feedback.

patterns.title: enemyGroup.clear(true, true) destroys sprites but leaks per-enemy Graphics children
patterns.description: Enemies often have an associated `enemy.graphics` Graphics object (drawn via createNeonGraphics) that follows the sprite position. The Graphics is NOT in the physics group — it's an independent display-list child. `enemyGroup.clear(true, true)` destroys the sprites but leaks the Graphics. Symptom: skipping waves with N leaves a snake's segments / pinwheel's spinning shape stuck on screen, accumulating each skip until the renderer chokes.
patterns.prompt_directive: When enemies have attached `.graphics`, `.segments[]`, `.graphics.glow`, or any other display-list children NOT in the enemy group, write a `_killEnemy(enemy)` helper that destroys all of them (with tween-kill first), and ALWAYS use that helper. Never trust `group.clear(true, true)` to clean up enemy auxiliary visuals.

patterns.title: Split-on-death debris that chases the player feels like a missile-guided bug
patterns.description: Pinwheel/jelly/cluster split-on-death pieces are usually thrown outward with initial velocity, but if their AI defaults to the 'chase player' case in the enemy switch, they curve back and home in like missiles. Players experience this as 'a glowing ball followed me across the screen and killed me.' Debris should DRIFT in their initial direction with mild deceleration and self-destruct after ~2.5s — pure hazards, not enemies.
patterns.prompt_directive: Split-on-death debris (pinwheel/jelly/cluster fragments) MUST use a dedicated `'drift'` enemyType that keeps initial outward velocity with mild deceleration (0.985 per tick) and self-destructs after 2.5s. Never let split-children fall through to a chase-player default — they read as missile-guided and feel buggy.

patterns.title: Text + setInteractive + backgroundColor padding has unreliable hit area
patterns.description: Text GameObjects with `backgroundColor` + `padding` look like buttons (rendered rectangle around text) but `setInteractive()` defaults to the TEXT GLYPH bounds, not the visible padded rect. Clicks on the colored padding miss; only clicks directly on letters register. The standard Phaser button pattern that ships with most tutorials suffers from this. Use `add.rectangle()` (which has a true bounded shape primitive) + an explicit `setInteractive(new Phaser.Geom.Rectangle(-w/2, -h/2, w, h), Phaser.Geom.Rectangle.Contains)`. For maximum reliability across scene transitions, combine THREE input paths: per-rectangle setInteractive, scene-level pointerdown with manual hit-test using `pointer.x/y`, and a native DOM mousedown listener on the canvas.
patterns.prompt_directive: For menu/game-over buttons NEVER use Text+backgroundColor+padding+setInteractive — the hit area is the glyph bounds, not the padded rectangle. Use `add.rectangle()` with explicit `setInteractive(new Phaser.Geom.Rectangle(...), Contains)`. For critical buttons (TRY AGAIN, START), add a redundant scene-level `input.on('pointerdown')` with manual hit-test AND a native DOM mousedown listener on `this.game.canvas` (cleaned up on scene shutdown). Plus an ENTER keyboard fallback.

patterns.title: Crossfading music during high-action moments triggers audio context stalls
patterns.description: A 'world transition' that swaps music tracks (fade-out old, fade-in new) sounds clean in isolation, but when the swap fires the same frame as a boss-clear cleanup (destroying many enemies + tweens + graphics), the Sound Manager can stall. The browser's audio context creates a new BufferSource on play, the old one's tween is mid-fade, and combined with renderer pressure, the game freezes. Either decouple music swaps from gameplay state changes (defer them to a quiet frame) or just don't swap music at all — let world identity live in enemy visuals + boss themes, not background tracks.
patterns.prompt_directive: Avoid fading + replacing background music during gameplay state transitions (wave advance, boss death, world clear). Either keep one track for the whole game (use enemy visuals to convey world identity) OR defer the music swap to a quiet frame via delayedCall. Never run a music tween-fade in the same frame as a wave-cleanup.

patterns.title: Default 960×540 canvas + Phaser.Scale.NONE leaves the game tiny on big monitors
patterns.description: Many tutorials set up a 960×540 game with no scale mode, so on a 1920×1080 monitor the canvas is tiny and centered. Switching to `Phaser.Scale.FIT` upscales the same 960×540 to 1920×1080 — that's a 2× bilinear stretch, looks pixel-y. The right pattern: set INTRINSIC resolution to 1280×720 (matches most laptops natively, and only 1.5× scale on 1080p fullscreen — significantly sharper) AND use Phaser.Scale.FIT with autoCenter, AND CSS-fill the parent container with `100vw / 100vh, position: fixed, inset: 0`.
patterns.prompt_directive: Default game resolution should be 1280×720 (NOT 960×540). Use `scale: { mode: Phaser.Scale.FIT, autoCenter: Phaser.Scale.CENTER_BOTH }` in the Phaser config. The parent container CSS must be viewport-filling (`position: fixed; inset: 0; width: 100vw; height: 100vh`) for the FIT mode to scale up correctly on large monitors and in fullscreen.
